elements.sent-2411-001: Based on current observations, our Universe is almost isotropic and presumably also homogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-002: It contains dark matter (DM) that interacts weakly with ordinary matter~\cite{Planck:2018vyg .}
elements.sent-2411-003: Inflation, where the Universe experiences a period of accelerated expansion, provides an excellent explanation to this isotropy and homogeneity~\cite{Linde:1981mu, Albrecht:1982wi .}
elements.sent-2411-004: The quantum fluctuations of inflaton field also seed the initial conditions for structure formation.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition, inflation can source particle production; one example is the so-called gravitational particle production (GPP), where time evolution of the background spacetime leads to the production~\cite{Lyth:1996yj,Chung:1998zb .}
elements.sent-2411-006: GPP is inevitable for any degree of freedom as long as it couples to gravity.
elements.sent-2411-007: This production channel can contribute significantly to dark matter abundance, and generate isocurvature perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-008: For a comprehensive review, we refer the review~\cite{Kolb:2023ydq .}
elements.sent-2411-009: If gravity is the only interaction between DM and Standard Model (SM) particles, DM from GPP will never be in thermal equilibrium with the rest of particles, unlike canonical thermal freeze-in or freeze-out mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-010: GPP has been extensively studied in the past two decades, leading to a rich phenomenology in scalar and higher spin fields~\cite{Lyth:1996yj, Chung:1998ua,Kolb:1998ki,Mukhanov:2005sc,Ling:2021zlj,Garcia:2023qab,Kuzmin:1998kk,Chung:2011ck,Graham:2015rva,Ahmed:2020fhc,Kolb:2020fwh,Gorghetto:2022sue,Cembranos:2023qph,Ozsoy:2023gnl,Hasegawa:2017hgd,Kallosh:1999jj,Antoniadis:2021jtg,Kaneta:2023uwi,Casagrande:2023fjk,Kolb:2023dzp,Nilles:2001ry,Nilles:2001fg .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Meanwhile, analytic treatments to track particle productions have been developed in certain cases~\cite{Chung:1998bt, Ema:2018ucl, Cembranos:2019qlm, Enomoto:2020xlf, Basso:2021whd,Hashiba:2021npn,Basso:2022tpd,Kaneta:2022gug,Racco:2024aac,Verner:2024agh,Jenks:2024fiu .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Even though the precise results of GPP depend on the inflation model, some general statements can be made without specifying inflation dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-013: For a scalar field $\chi$ lighter than the inflation scale $H_I$, its number density spectrum will feature an infra-red (IR) divergence, which has to be regulated through a cut-off in momentum space.
elements.sent-2411-014: Its isocurvature spectrum will be almost scale invariant, which might go conflicts with CMB observations.
elements.sent-2411-015: On the contrast, if the field is heavier than $H_I$, its number density will be convergent without cut-off and the isocurvature signal can be negligible.
elements.sent-2411-016: Due to the weak nature of gravity, the above conclusion can be easily modified.
elements.sent-2411-017: Corrections may appear from various sources.
elements.sent-2411-018: Introducing non-minimal coupling between DM and gravity can greatly change the efficiency of GPP.
elements.sent-2411-019: A conformal coupled scalar experiences less GPP, has no IR divergence and leaves negligible isocurvature at CMB scale, larger interaction will enhance the GPP production~\cite{Markkanen:2015xuw, Fairbairn:2018bsw, Cembranos:2019qlm, Clery:2022wib, Garcia:2023qab, Yu:2023ity .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Introducing direct coupling between inflaton field and DM leads to a similar effects~\cite{Garcia:2022vwm,Garcia:2023awt .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper we would like to focus on another possibility, where such correction comes from supergravity (SUGRA) effects.
elements.sent-2411-022: SUGRA is the local gauge theory of supersymmetry (SUSY), which generally predicts non-renormalizable interactions between inflaton field and DM field.
elements.sent-2411-023: These interactions can play a significant role in cosmology~\cite{Endo:2006qk,Endo:2006nj,Endo:2007sz,Enomoto:2013mla,Ema:2024sit .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Previous study in SUGRA GPP focused on the case where $m_\chi> H_I$~\cite{Nakayama:2019yjv , and we would like to have a comprehensive understanding over different parameter spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Even for light DMs, SUGRA correction makes their effective mass naturally larger than Hubble scale.
elements.sent-2411-026: This correction has the same origin as the $\eta$ problem in SUGRA inflation research~\cite{Copeland:1994vg .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In this paper, we focus on $\alpha$-attractor model, which is realized in SUGRA using a stabilizer field~\cite{DallAgata:2014qsj,Kallosh:2014hxa .}
elements.sent-2411-028: This framework also allows us to introduce arbitrary SUSY breaking scale $m_{3/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Even though for canonical choice of $m_{3/2}\sim TeV$, its impact on GPP can be safely neglect.
elements.sent-2411-030: It is still interesting to investigate how a larger $m_{3/2}$ could alert GPP.
elements.sent-2411-031: We numerically calculate the number density of a scalar DM, as well as its isocurvature contribution.
elements.sent-2411-032: Compared with non-SUSY GPP, SUGRA models need a higher reheating temperature to produce the same amount of DM.
elements.sent-2411-033: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-034: In section \ref{sec:theory , we introduce the inflation model, basics of GPP, and eventually how to embed our model in SUGRA framework.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The numerical results of GPP are presented in section \ref{sec:num and possibly observational consequences are discussed.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Section \ref{sec:conc contains conclusion and outlook of this work.}
elements.section-2411-002: Theoretical framework of GPP and SUGRA GPP
elements.sent-2411-037: Inflation is assumed to be a period of quasi-de Sitter spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-038: The simplest realization is single-field slow-roll inflation, where the assumed (scalar) field slows down dramatically.
elements.sent-2411-039: The kinetic energy is negligible compared to the potential energy and therefore the equation of state becomes $\omega \approx -1$.
elements.sent-2411-040: This leads the Universe to expand exponentially.
elements.sent-2411-041: The equation of motion of the inflaton field and Friedmann equations of first and second kind expressed in conformal time $\tau$ are \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\phi '' + 2 H \phi' + a^2 \pdv{V}{\phi} = 0, H^2 = \left( \frac{a'}{a} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \left( \frac{1}{2} \phi'^2 + a^2 V \right), \frac{a''}{a} = \frac{1}{6 \mpl^2} \left(4a^2 V - \phi'^2 \right),\end{equation} \end{subequations where prime denotes derivative with respect to the conformal time.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\phi '' + 2 H \phi' + a^2 \pdv{V}{\phi} = 0, H^2 = \left( \frac{a'}{a} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \left( \frac{1}{2} \phi'^2 + a^2 V \right), \frac{a''}{a} = \frac{1}{6 \mpl^2} \left(4a^2 V - \phi'^2 \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-042: The (potential) slow-roll parameters are given by the first and second derivative of the inflaton potential \begin{equation}\epsilon_V = \frac{1}{2 \mpl^2} \left( \frac{V'}{V}\right)^2, \quad \eta_V = \frac{1}{\mpl^2} \frac{V''}{V}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: It can be shown slow-roll inflation happens when $\epsilon, |\eta_V| \ll 1$.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this work, we choose to work with T model $\alpha$-attractor as our inflation model, which can be easily realized in the SUGRA context, see section \ref{sec:SUGRA .}
elements.sent-2411-046: It has the following potential \cite{Kallosh:2013yoa \begin{equation}V_I(\phi)= V_0 \tanh^{2n}{\left(\frac{\phi}{\sqrt{6\alpha}}\right)}. \label{eq:t-mode-potential}\end{equation} In this work, $n=1$ is used throughout.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The slow roll parameters for this model are \begin{equation}\epsilon_V = \frac{2n^2}{\sinh(\phi/\sqrt{6\alpha}) \cosh(\phi / \sqrt{6\alpha})} \eta_V = \frac{1}{6\alpha} \frac{2(1-\tanh^2(\phi/\sqrt{6\alpha})) - 6\tanh^2(\phi/\sqrt{6\alpha}) (1-\tanh^2(\phi/\sqrt{6\alpha}))}{\tanh^2(\phi/\sqrt{6\alpha})}\end{equation} The spectral index of the scalar perturbation $n_s$ can be given by the two potential slow-roll parameters at the CMB scale \begin{equation}n_s - 1 = 2 \eta_* - 4 \epsilon_*.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: It has to be consistent with the observed value of $n_s = 0.965 \pm 0.004$ \cite{Planck:2018vyg .}
elements.sent-2411-049: One can use this to compute the field value $\phi_*$ at the CMB scale \cite{Germ_n_2021 \begin{equation}\cosh^2 \left(\frac{\phi_*}{\sqrt{6\alpha}}\right) = \frac{1}{2(1-n_s)} \left(1-n_s + \frac{4}{3\alpha} + \sqrt{(1-n_s)^2 + \frac{8}{3\alpha}(1-n_s) + \frac{16}{9\alpha^2} } \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We can rewrite the potential parameter $\alpha$ in terms of observables.
elements.sent-2411-051: When fixing the tensor-to-scalar ratio and spectral index, we have \begin{equation}\alpha = \frac{64}{3} \frac{r}{(8(1-n_s)-r)^2 - r^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: As we only have a upper bound on $r$, $\alpha$ also only has a upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-053: At last, the ``scale'' of the potential $V_0$ can be determined by the normalization of the power spectrum \cite{Planck:2018jri \begin{equation}\mathcal{A} = \frac{1}{24\pi^2} \left.\frac{V}{\epsilon}\right|_{\phi=\phi_*} \stackrel{!}{=} 2.1 \cdot 10^{-9}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Another important scale is the infalton mass, which reads \begin{equation}m_\phi^2 = \left. \pdv[2]{V}{\phi} \right|_{\phi=0} = \frac{V_0}{3\alpha}. \label{eq:Tphimass}\end{equation} As a result, we have in this model $m_\phi^2 / H_\text{inf ^2 = 1/\alpha$ which can be much smaller than unity, especially if the bound for tensor-to-scalar ratio is lowered in the future.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The end of slow-roll inflation is marked by either one of the magnitude of slow roll parameters being unity \begin{equation}2\cosh^2 \left(\frac{\phi_e}{\sqrt{6 \alpha}} \right) = \begin{cases} 1 + \frac{2}{3\alpha} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{8}{3\alpha}\left( \frac{1}{6\alpha} - 1 \right)}, \alpha \leq \alpha_l 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{4}{3\alpha}} , \alpha > \alpha_l \end{cases}, \label{eq:end_of_tmode_inflation}\end{equation} with $\alpha_l \approx 0.17$ for $n=1$.

u} \partial_\mu \chi \partial_

u}$ and $R$ is the Ricci scalar.
elements.sent-2411-057: Higher order self-interactions of $\chi$ have been neglected and $m_\chi^2$ could be a function of other fields.
elements.sent-2411-058: Here, we use the FLRW metric with conformal time \begin{equation}g_{\mu\nu} \dd{x^\mu} \dd{x^\nu} = a^2(\tau) \left( \dd{\tau}^2 - \dd{\vec{x}^2} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, after Fourier decomposing the field $\tilde{\chi}$, the equation of motion for $\tilde{\chi}$ field turns into the equation for the mode function \begin{equation}\tilde{\chi}_k '' + \omega^2_k \tilde{\chi}_k = 0, \quad \omega_k^2 = k^2 + a^2 m_{\chi, \text{eff}}^2 = k^2 + a^2 m_{\chi}^2 - (1-6\xi) \frac{a''}{a}. \label{eq:mode-eq}\end{equation} In this work, we only consider vanishing conformal coupling $\xi = 0$.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\tilde{\chi}_k '' + \omega^2_k \tilde{\chi}_k = 0, \quad \omega_k^2 = k^2 + a^2 m_{\chi, eff}^2 = k^2 + a^2 m_{\chi}^2 - (1-6\xi) \frac{a''}{a}. \label{eq:mode-eq}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-061: In the case where $\omega^2_k$ varies rapidly, the field $\chi$ can get excited and particles are produced.
elements.sent-2411-062: The same phenomenon can also be understood in terms of the Bogoliubov coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-063: Assume that the spacetime are asymptotically flat at early and late times.
elements.sent-2411-064: The Bogoliubov coefficients describes how the orthonormal basis functions at these times relate to each other.
elements.sent-2411-065: The equation \eqref{eq:mode-eq can be formulated in terms of the Bogoliubov coefficients \begin{equation}\label{eq:bogoliubov} \alpha'_k v_k = \frac{\omega'_k}{2\omega_k} v_k^* \beta_k, \quad \beta'_k v_k^* = \frac{\omega'_k}{2\omega_k} v_k \alpha_k,\end{equation} with \begin{equation}v_k(\tau) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\omega_k}} \exp(-i \int \omega_k \dd{\tau}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: It proves to be more efficient numerically to solve for these instead of the mode functions\footnote{Only exception is when $\omega^2$ turns negative.. .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The number density spectrum is given by \begin{equation}f_\chi(k,\tau) = |\beta(k,\tau)|^2,\end{equation} and the comoving number density can be obtained from it \begin{equation}{a}^3 n_\chi = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \int \dd{k}k^2 |\beta_k|^2. \label{eq:number-density}\end{equation} We assume that the comoving number density doesn't change after production and they are always the cold dark matter \footnote{Note that since we take $a_{e m_\phi$ as the unit of comoving momenta, the LHS is then $\left(\frac{a {a_e \right)^3$ .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Using normal ordering to cure the UV divergence, the final energy density can be approximated as \begin{equation}a^3 \rho_\chi \approx \frac{1}{ 2 \pi^2 a^4} \int \dd k k^2 |\beta_k|^2 \omega_k ,\end{equation} where we only include terms up to second order in $|\beta_k|$ and ignore the terms suppressed by Hubble parameter and Ricci scalar (decays away quickly).
elements.sent-2411-069: The isocurvature power spectrum is defined as \begin{equation}\Delta_\delta^2(k) = \frac{1}{\rho^2} \frac{k^3}{2\pi^2} \int \dd[3]{r} e^{-i \vec{k}\cdot \vec{r}} \expval{\delta \hat{\rho} (0) \delta\hat{\rho}( \vec{r}) },\end{equation} where time-dependence has been ignored.
elements.sent-2411-070: It can be written in terms of the mode functions \cite{Chung_2005, Ling:2021zlj, Kolb:2023ydq \newcommand{\kPrimek {{|\pmb{k '-\pmb{k | \begin{equation} \Delta_\delta^2(\eta, k) = \frac{1}{a^8 \bar{\rho}^2} \frac{k^3}{(2\pi)^5} \int \dd[3]{k'} \Big[ |\partial\chi_{k'}|^2 |\partial\chi_\kPrimek|^2 + a^4 m^4 |\chi_{k'}|^2 |\chi_\kPrimek|^2 \quad + a^2m^2 \left( \chi_{k'} \partial\chi_{k'}^* \chi_\kPrimek \partial\chi_\kPrimek^* + \chi_{k'}^* \partial\chi_{k'} \chi_\kPrimek^* \partial\chi_\kPrimek \right) \Big] \end{equation} Instead, it can be reformulated using the Bogoliubov coefficients and momenta can be relabeled at $(k, k', |\pmb{k '-\pmb{k |) \rightarrow (k, p, q)$ \cite{Garcia:2023awt \begin{equation}\Delta_\delta^2(k) = \frac{a_e^2 m_\chi^2}{ (a_e^4 \rho)^2} \frac{2k^2}{(2\pi)^4} \int_0^\infty \dd{p} p \int_{|k-p|}^{k+p} \dd{q} q \left[ |\beta_p|^2 |\alpha_q|^2 + \Re{\alpha_p^* \beta_p \alpha_q \beta_q^* e^{2i\Phi_p - 2i \Phi_q}} \right] \label{eq:iso_formula}\end{equation} where the time-dependence has been omitted as we are only interested in the final spectrum.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this section we will discuss how to realize inflation in supergravity framework.
elements.sent-2411-072: In supergravity, scalar potential is generated by a K\"ahler potential $\mathcal{K}$ and Superpotential $\mathcal{W}$, both of them are functions of superfields $\Phi^\alpha$, where $\alpha$ refers to different superfields in the theory.
elements.sent-2411-073: Note K\"ahler potential $\mathcal{K}$ is a hermitian function while superpotential $\mathcal{W}$ is a holomorphic function.
elements.sent-2411-074: In the scalar part, K\"ahler potential determines the kinetic function of scalar fields and controls the scalar potential together with superpotential (We use Planck units in this section $\mpl=1$). \begin{equation}\begin{split} \sqrt{-g}\mathcal{L} = -\mathcal{K}_{\alpha\overline{\beta}}\partial_\mu \phi^\alpha \partial^\mu \Bar{\phi}^{\overline{\beta}} - V V = e^{{\cal K}}({\cal K}^{\alpha\overline{\beta}}D_{\alpha}{\cal W}\overline{D_{\beta}{\cal W}} - 3|{\cal W}|^2)\,, \end{split}\end{equation} where $\phi^\alpha$ is the spin-0 component of superfield $\Phi^\alpha$.
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} \sqrt{-g}L = -K_{\alpha\overline{\beta}}\partial_\mu \phi^\alpha \partial^\mu \Bar{\phi}^{\overline{\beta}} - V V = e^{{\cal K}}({\cal K}^{\alpha\overline{\beta}}D_{\alpha}{\cal W}\overline{D_{\beta}{\cal W}} - 3|{\cal W}|^2)\,, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: Existence of $e^{\mathcal{K }$ in the scalar potential leads to the so called $\eta$ problem in the early study of supergravity inflation~\cite{Copeland:1994vg .}}
elements.sent-2411-076: For any field with canonical K\"ahler potential $\mathcal{K =\Bar{\Phi}\Phi$, the second order derivative of the potential: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi \partial \phi} \sim V + others...\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-077: This holds true for both possible inflaton field and any other fields present in the K\"ahler potential.
elements.sent-2411-078: In this paper, we will assume this correction indeed exist for dark matter field, which means DM get a effective mass in order of Hubble scale.
elements.sent-2411-079: As we will show in this paper, this significantly alter the results for GPP production of DM fields.
elements.sent-2411-080: One elegant solution of $\eta$ problem is shift symmetry, by which the inflaton field doesn't appear in the K\"ahler potential.
elements.sent-2411-081: In this way, one can realize most of the inflation models~\cite{Kallosh:2010xz .}
elements.sent-2411-082: To embed the aforementioned inflation model in supergravity, we use the formalism proposed in~\cite{DallAgata:2014qsj,Kallosh:2014hxa , which can accommodate arbitrary inflationary model with arbitrary supersymmetry (SUSY) breaking scale.}
elements.sent-2411-083: It essentially has three superfields: the inflaton field $\Phi$, the stabilizer field $S$ and the DM field $\chi$, with the following K\"ahler potential $\mathcal{K}$ and Superpotential $\mathcal{W}$ \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathcal{K}(\Phi,S,\chi) = -\frac{1}{2}(\Phi-\Bar{\Phi})^2+S\Bar{S}+\chi\Bar{\chi}, \mathcal{W}(\Phi,S,\chi) = f(\Phi)+ Sg(\Phi)+\frac{1}{2}m_\chi\chi^2. \end{split}\end{equation} We also assume both $Im(\Phi),S,\chi=0$ during inflation as in~\cite{Kallosh:2010xz .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The K\"ahler covariant derivative of each field reads \begin{equation}\begin{split} D_\Phi W = f'(\Phi)+g'(\Phi) S- (\Phi-\Bar{\Phi})W, D_S W =g(\Phi)+\Bar{S}W, D_\chi W =m_\chi\chi+\bar{\chi}W. \end{split}\end{equation} We set $g(\Phi)=\sqrt{3 f(\Phi)$ and the scalar potential reads \begin{equation}V=e^{K} \left( K^{\alpha \Bar{\beta}}{ D_\alpha W }\overline{D_\beta W} -3 \abs{W}^2 \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation} D_\Phi W = f'(\Phi)+g'(\Phi) S- (\Phi-\Bar{\Phi})W, D_S W =g(\Phi)+\Bar{S}W, D_\chi W =m_\chi\chi+\bar{\chi}W. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: Up to second order in $\chi$ and ignore the contribution form $Im(\Phi)$ and $S$, we have \begin{equation}\begin{split} V_\chi=e^K \Big( m_\chi^2\chi \bar{\chi}+\frac{1}{4} m_\chi^2 \chi^2 \Bar{\chi}^2+\frac{1}{4} m_\chi^2 \chi^3 \Bar{\chi}^3 -\frac{1}{2} m_\chi\Bar{\chi}^2f +\frac{1}{2}m_\chi \chi \Bar{\chi}^3 f -\frac{1}{2} m_\chi \chi^2 \Bar{f}+\frac{1}{2}m_\chi \chi^3 \bar{\chi}\Bar{f} +\chi \Bar{\chi} f \Bar{f}+f' \Bar{f'} \Big), \end{split}\end{equation} where $V_I(\phi)=\abs{f'(\Phi) ^2$ is the inflationary potential and $\phi=\sqrt{2 Re(\Phi)$ is the actual inflaton.}}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation} V_\chi=e^K \Big( m_\chi^2\chi \bar{\chi}+\frac{1}{4} m_\chi^2 \chi^2 \Bar{\chi}^2+\frac{1}{4} m_\chi^2 \chi^3 \Bar{\chi}^3 -\frac{1}{2} m_\chi\Bar{\chi}^2f +\frac{1}{2}m_\chi \chi \Bar{\chi}^3 f -\frac{1}{2} m_\chi \chi^2 \Bar{f}+\frac{1}{2}m_\chi \chi^3 \bar{\chi}\Bar{f} +\chi \Bar{\chi} f \Bar{f}+f' \Bar{f'} \Big), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: Accordingly, the inflationary potential \eqref{eq:t-mode-potential has the following $f$ function (with $n=1$) \begin{equation}f(\Phi) = \sqrt{3\alpha V_0} \log\left(\cosh(\frac{\Phi} {\sqrt{3\alpha}})\right)+\frac{m_{3/2}}{\sqrt{3}}. \label{eq:func_f}\end{equation} In terms of DM field, We can split the complex field into two real fields $\chi = (\chi_R + i \chi_I) / \sqrt{2}$ with the following mass term: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}m_{\chi, R}^2 = m_\chi^2 + V_I + |f|^2 - m_\chi f, m_{\chi, I}^2 = m_\chi^2 + V_I + |f|^2 + m_\chi f,\end{equation} \end{subequations where we have used $f \in \R$ as only $Re(\Phi)$ is non-zero and there is no mixing between the real and imaginary parts.}}}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}m_{\chi, R}^2 = m_\chi^2 + V_I + |f|^2 - m_\chi f, m_{\chi, I}^2 = m_\chi^2 + V_I + |f|^2 + m_\chi f,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-087: After the rescaling $\tilde{\chi _i = a \chi_i$, their effective masses read \begin{equation}\meff^2 = a^2 \left( m_\chi^2 + H^2 + |f|^2 \mp m_\chi f \right). \label{eq:m_eff}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-088: In this section we show the necessary numerical results for calculation of DM relic abundance in the current universe.
elements.sent-2411-089: The first step is to solve the background evolution of inflaton field through equations \eqref{math:background-eq .}
elements.sent-2411-090: In practice, we use the slow-roll solution as initial conditions for the inflaton field.
elements.sent-2411-091: As the inflation model is an attractor, the trajectory converges to the ``true'' one after sufficient time.
elements.sent-2411-092: By choosing the above condition for the background, we also need to ensure that every mode is well inside the horizon upon simulation start and can be described by the Bunch-Davies vacuum \begin{equation}\chi_k = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \omega_k}}, \; \chi'_k = \frac{-i \omega_k}{\sqrt{2\omega_k}}, \quad \text{or equivalently,} \quad \alpha = 1, \; \beta = 0,\end{equation} where $\omega_k$ is taken at the time of initialization and it approaches $k$ in the infinite past $\tau \rightarrow -\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-093: This means longer wavelength modes need to be initialized earlier.
elements.sent-2411-094: The smallest scale considered here is $k = 10^{-2 \cdot a_e H_e$, and it requires initialization at $\sim 10$ e-folds before the end of inflation.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figs/TMode-0.0035/background_draft.pdf} \caption{Background evolution: inflaton field value $\phi$, Hubble parameter $H$ and Ricci scalar $R$, with $\alpha \approx 1.0$ corresponding to would-be tensor to scalar ratio $r=0.0035$. The time variable has been transformed to number of $e$-folds $N(t) = \ln(a(t))$.} \label{fig:background-plots}
elements.sent-2411-095: We show the relevant background quantities in figure \ref{fig:background-plots .}
elements.sent-2411-096: This is done using the inflation potential given in equation \eqref{eq:t-mode-potential with a fixed tensor to scalar ratio $r=0.0035$ ($\alpha \approx 1$).}
elements.sent-2411-097: When plotting the variables, we use the number of $e$-folds $N = \ln a(t)$, instead of the cosmic time $t$, as the time variable.
elements.sent-2411-098: The scale factor has been rescaled so that the scale factor at end of inflation is unity $a(t_e) \equiv 1$, which was chosen by the condition that the first Hubble slow-roll parameter equals $0.1$ \begin{equation}\epsilon_1 = - \frac{\dot{H}}{H^2} = \frac{1}{2\mpl^2} \left( \dv{\phi}{N} \right)^2 \stackrel{!}{=} 0.1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-099: Thus, the number of $e$-folds at the end of inflation is defined as zero.
elements.sent-2411-100: The first diagram shows the evolution of the field value $\phi(N)$.
elements.sent-2411-101: It smoothly rolls down to the minimum at $\phi=0$ and starts to oscillate around it.
elements.sent-2411-102: This oscillation process induces the decrease of Hubble scale $H$ as well as an oscillation of the Ricci scalar $R$.
elements.sent-2411-103: During slow roll inflation, both two quantities are more or less constants: \begin{equation}\label{math:ricci_inf} R = - 6 \frac{a''}{a^3} \approx -12 H^2.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-199: In this section we briefly review the thermal history of our universe and explain how we calculate the density to entropy ratio explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-200: Our simulation starts when the inflaton $\phi$ field stays in the inflation region, which we will formally denote it by $t_{-\infty}$.
elements.sent-2411-201: At $t=t_{e}$ the (Hubble) slow roll conditions are broken and $\phi$ field start to oscillate around its minimum.
elements.sent-2411-202: In this paper we consider the case where the oscillation part can be approximated by $\phi^2$, which means the energy density of the inflaton field red-shift as matter.
elements.sent-2411-203: This transition from de Sitter spacetime to matter dominated space also triggers the gravitational production of the super heavy dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-204: Most of the super heavy dark matter are produced at the beginning of the oscillation part, and we assume at $t=t_p$ this production is basically complete and its energy density also red-shifts as matter.
elements.sent-2411-205: Hence we have the following identity \begin{equation}\frac{\rho_\chi(t_p)}{\rho_\phi(t_p)}=\frac{\rho_\chi(t_{rh})}{\rho_\phi(t_{rh})}=\frac{\rho_\chi(t_{rh})}{\rho_r(t_{rh})},\end{equation} where we have used that dark matter $\chi$ field redshifts in the same way as the inflaton $\phi$ field, and we define the reheating temperature when $\rho_\phi(t_{rh )=\rho_r(t_{rh )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Here $\rho_r$ denotes the energy density in radiation.
elements.sent-2411-207: After the reheating is complete, we have \begin{equation}\frac{\rho_\chi(t_{rh})}{\rho_r(t_{rh})}=\frac{\rho_\chi(t_0)}{\rho_r(t_0)}\frac{T_0}{T_rh}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-208: The entropy density and energy density in today's Universe reads \begin{equation}\begin{split} s_0 =g_*\frac{2\pi^2}{45}T_0^3, \rho_{r,0} =g_*\frac{\pi^2}{30}T_0^4. \end{split}\end{equation} Hence, we can rewrite $\rho_{r,0 =\frac{3 {4 sT_0$ and the energy density to entropy density reads \begin{equation}\frac{\rho_\chi(t_0)}{s_0}=\frac{3}{4}T_0\frac{\rho_\chi(t_0)}{\rho_r(t_0)}=\frac{3 m_\chi T_{rh}}{4}\frac{n_\chi(t_p)}{\rho_\phi(t_p)},\end{equation} where we have used $\rho=m n$ for non-relativistic particles (CDM).}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: The energy density of $\rho_\phi(t_p)$ can be connected to the energy density of inflaton at the end of inflation \begin{equation}\rho_\phi(t_p)=\rho_{\phi} (t_{e})\left(\frac{a({t_e})}{a(t_p)}\right)^3=3\mpl^2 H^2(t_{e})\left(\frac{a({t_e})}{a(t_p)}\right)^3.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-210: The finial expression reads \begin{equation}\frac{\rho_\chi(t_0)}{s_0} =\frac{ m_\chi T_{rh} H(t_e)}{4\mpl^2}\left(\frac{a^3(t_p) n_\chi(t_p)}{a^3({t_e}) H^3(t_e)}\right) . \label{eq:rho_s0}\end{equation} Current observation suggests this quantity should be equal to $\SI{4e-10 {\giga \electronvolt}$ to contribute the full amount of dark matter.}

elements.sent-2411-212: Based on current observations, on large scale our Universe is almost isotropic and presumably also homogeneous.
elements.sent-2411-213: It contains dark matter (DM) that interacts weakly with the ordinary matter~\cite{Planck:2018vyg .}

elements.sent-2411-216: In addition, inflation can source particle production; one example is the so-called gravitational particle production (GPP), due to the time evolution of the background spacetime~\cite{Lyth:1996yj,Chung:1998zb .}
elements.sent-2411-217: This production channel can contribute significantly to the DM abundance, and can generate subsequently DM isocurvature perturbations.

elements.sent-2411-277: In this section we discuss how to realize inflation in a SUGRA framework.

elements.sent-2411-279: Both of them are functions of chiral superfields $\Phi^\alpha$, where $\alpha$ refers to different superfields in the theory.
elements.sent-2411-280: Note that the K\"ahler potential $\mathcal{K}$ is a hermitian function while the superpotential $\mathcal{W}$ is a holomorphic function.
